summary:
The retrieved results do not provide direct evidence linking 2-methoxyestradiol specifically to reduced chemotherapy dosing in ovarian cancer. While hormone therapies may offer benefits in certain subtypes like low-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer, none of these findings pertain to 2-methoxyestradiol’s role in modifying drug regimens or lowering chemo doses. Thus, despite general interest in estrogenic compounds' effects on tumor biology, no supporting data exist showing efficacy in decreasing standard-of-care chemotherapy requirements due to combined administration with 2-methoxyestradiol.\boxed{B}

Only consider the latest round where actual tool calls occurred; ignore earlier rounds unless they contain updated conclusions.

Based solely upon the newly gathered evidence:

Current status after retrieving 32 related articles:  
Despite extensive search efforts yielding multiple references concerning estradiols and hormonal management strategies—particularly focusing on outcomes post-platinum-based chemotherapy—the corpus contains zero explicit mentions connecting either 2-methoxyestradiol alone or in combination with conventional agents toward any measurable decrease in administered chemotherapy intensity or volume across patient populations diagnosed with human ovarian cancers.
